metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how the trusted rebar-mcp server exposes an HTTP endpoint for SigV4-authenticated callers: either scope adding an HTTP/SSE transport to rebar-mcp (FastMCP supports `streamable_http`), or explicitly adopt the library-mode service pattern (as in `review_bot/app.py` and ADR-0009) where rebar is imported as a library behind a thin HTTP handler — and record this transport-layer decision in child 4's scope.
findings.finding: The plan describes the trusted rebar-mcp server as authenticating via 'AWS IAM (SigV4-signed requests)' over an HTTP endpoint, but the existing rebar-mcp server is exclusively a stdio transport (confirmed: mcp_server.py `main()` calls `build_server().run()` with no HTTP/SSE transport, and its `--help` text reads 'FastMCP, stdio transport'). SigV4 is an HTTP request-signing protocol — it cannot authenticate stdio messages. The plan never specifies adding an HTTP transport to rebar-mcp; child 4 would build an AWS deployment against a server that has no mechanism to receive or authenticate HTTP requests.
findings.location: AC5 ('The AWS MCP-server infra authenticates its endpoint via AWS IAM (SigV4-signed requests)') and Scope/child-4 ('Trusted rebar-mcp remote-execution path ... its AWS infra (IAM SigV4 endpoint auth)')
findings.suggested_fix: In child 4's scope, add an explicit deliverable: 'Add HTTP transport to rebar-mcp (FastMCP streamable_http mode) OR implement the trusted server as a library-mode HTTP service (per ADR-0009 pattern) behind an AWS API Gateway; the choice determines the SigV4 integration point.' The existing review_bot pattern (rebar imported as library, not stdio MCP) is already proven and avoids transport-layer work.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the SigV4 endpoint-authentication mechanism in the plan: name the specific enforcement layer (e.g., AWS API Gateway with IAM authorizer, ALB IAM auth) that will validate SigV4-signed requests to the rebar-mcp HTTP endpoint, and confirm this is the established AWS prior-art approach for custom-backend IAM auth.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'AWS IAM (SigV4-signed requests)' for rebar-mcp endpoint authentication but never names the enforcement mechanism that would actually validate SigV4 signatures on a custom server. SigV4 is AWS's signing protocol for AWS-managed service APIs; a standalone custom server does not natively receive or verify SigV4-signed requests without an explicit intermediary (API Gateway with IAM authorizer, ALB IAM auth, or custom verification middleware). The existing mcp_server.py is stdio-only (line 285: 'FastMCP, stdio transport') and the existing infra/terraform/ has no API Gateway, ALB, or SigV4 verification resource. This is an unresolved capability assertion with well-established prior art the plan should name.
findings.location: AC5 (trusted rebar-mcp remote path) / Approach: 'Trusted remote execution for high assurance' / Intended decomposition child 4
findings.suggested_fix: Name AWS API Gateway (REST or HTTP) with `AWS_IAM` authorization as the SigV4 enforcement layer — this is the canonical AWS prior-art pattern for protecting a custom backend with IAM SigV4, requires no bespoke SigV4 verification code, and integrates cleanly with Terraform (`aws_api_gateway_rest_api` + `aws_api_gateway_authorizer` type `AWS_IAM`). Add a note that clients sign requests using boto3/botocore `AWS4Auth` (or equivalent) against the gateway endpoint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the HTTP transport mechanism for the trusted rebar-mcp server (e.g. FastMCP `streamable_http` transport mode or a separate ASGI adapter) and name the concrete SigV4 enforcement layer (API Gateway IAM authorizer, ALB with IAM auth, or custom middleware) so child 4 has a fully scoped integration surface before build-out begins.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'AWS IAM (SigV4-signed requests)' endpoint authentication for the trusted rebar-mcp server, but rebar-mcp is a stdio-only server (confirmed: mcp_server.py line 285 'FastMCP, stdio transport'; build_server().run() with no transport argument). Exposing rebar-mcp over HTTP requires either switching FastMCP to its streamable_http transport or standing up a separate HTTP adapter — neither is specified. SigV4 enforcement on a custom HTTP endpoint further requires a named enforcement layer (AWS API Gateway with IAM authorizer, ALB with IAM auth, or custom SigV4-verification middleware); no such layer is named in the plan and none exists in infra/terraform (confirmed: zero hits for aws_api_gateway, SigV4 in infra/). This is an unverified, multi-component integration with no codebase precedent.
findings.location: AC5 / 'Trusted remote execution for high assurance' (Approach section) / Intended decomposition child 4
findings.suggested_fix: Name the enforcement layer in AC5 and child 4's scope. The lightest-weight option consistent with existing infra patterns is AWS API Gateway (HTTP API) with an IAM authorizer in front of the server — this keeps SigV4 enforcement in AWS-managed infrastructure and avoids implementing custom request-verification code. Add a one-sentence IaC note: 'API Gateway HTTP API with IAM authorizer routes to the rebar-mcp HTTP endpoint; client signs requests with SigV4 using boto3 + a named IAM role.' Alternatively, if the intent is to reuse the existing EC2 deployment, name ALB with IAM auth instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the SigV4-over-stdio gap in child ee0b AC2: specify the AWS service (e.g. API Gateway + Lambda, or ALB with IAM auth) that terminates SigV4 requests and proxies to the MCP process, or replace SigV4 with a feasible equivalent (e.g. instance-identity-document validation, mTLS).
findings.finding: Child ee0b requires 'AWS IAM (SigV4-signed requests) restricted to a named admin role' as rebar-mcp endpoint authentication, but rebar-mcp is architecturally a stdio-only transport — there is no HTTP server endpoint over which SigV4 authentication could be enforced. The child repeats the parent's requirement without resolving this structural gap: no child specifies what component enforces SigV4 (an API Gateway, an ALB, a Lambda wrapper, etc.), leaving the endpoint-auth requirement with no implementable enforcement mechanism.
findings.location: child ee0b-c5e2-3454-42f1, AC2(a)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child ee0b context or AC2: 'The rebar-mcp trusted server exposes an HTTP endpoint via [API Gateway / ALB] that enforces IAM SigV4 before proxying to the MCP stdio process; the IaC declares this gateway resource and its IAM authorization policy.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC3 exactly where the CI-trigger classification comment must live (e.g., 'as a YAML comment block at the top of `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml`') and optionally add a test assertion (e.g., `grep` for the classification tag) so it is durable and verifiable.
findings.finding: The CI-trigger audit instruction in AC3 says 'a comment records the classification' but does not specify WHERE that comment lives. An executor could reasonably put it in the workflow YAML as an inline comment, in a separate markdown doc, in the PR description, or in a code review checklist — with no way to tell which is correct, and no test to enforce it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3, CI-trigger audit paragraph
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'a comment records the classification' with 'a YAML comment block at the top of `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml` records each trigger's classification (INCLUDED or NO_FILTER) with a one-line rationale; a test asserts the comment block is present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `ensure_ascii=False` to the `json.dumps` call in child 368c's material_fingerprint spec (AC2) to match the live gate implementation in `pass1.py` line 561.
findings.finding: Child 368c's AC2 specification of the material_fingerprint computation omits `ensure_ascii=False`, diverging from the live implementation in `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/pass1.py` line 561 (`json.dumps(basis, sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=False)`). An implementer following the child spec verbatim would produce a different fingerprint for any non-ASCII ticket content, breaking interoperability with existing gate fingerprints and defeating the replay/mutation detection AC.
findings.location: child 368c-1c88-3999-4fbd, AC2 — material fingerprint definition
findings.suggested_fix: Update child 368c AC2 to read: 'sha256(json.dumps({ticket_id, description, file_impact, children}, sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=False))' — matching the live gate fingerprint exactly.

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in AC3 whether op-cert grandfathering reuses the existing `ENFORCE_SINCE` repo variable (shared cutoff) or introduces a distinct variable (name it, e.g. `OPCERT_ENFORCE_SINCE`), and justify the choice relative to the different subjects (event files vs. certificates).
findings.finding: The `ENFORCE_SINCE`-style grandfathering mechanism is referenced for the op-cert CI lane ('ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering') but the plan never specifies whether this reuses the same `ENFORCE_SINCE` repo variable already consumed by `verify-identity.yaml` for authorship, or whether a distinct variable/config key is needed. The two gates have different scope (authorship events vs. op-cert certificates) so a shared cutoff may be semantically incorrect; a separate variable has no defined name or placement. This is an ambiguous counter/state-placement gap.
findings.location: AC3 / 'extends the shipped verify-identity.yaml merge-gate (same merged-log walk + ENFORCE_SINCE-style grandfathering)' / .github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml

findings.finding: The deploy ordering for the HMAC expand→contract migration is unspecified relative to the CI merge-gate and rebar-mcp server going live: if the CI gate begins enforcing asymmetric certs (child 3) before all existing environments have re-issued their HMAC certs (child 6 contract phase), tickets carrying HMAC op-certs in the merged log will be rejected mid-migration. The plan names the expand-contract artifact but never specifies which phase must be fully deployed before the next begins.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — HMAC removal AC; Intended decomposition children 3, 6
findings.suggested_fix: In the migration artifact outline (`docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md`) and child 6 AC, add an ordered checklist: Phase 1 — deploy asymmetric signing + verifier (HMAC still accepted); Phase 2 — all environments re-issue HMAC certs as asymmetric (operator confirms); Phase 3 — enable CI gate asymmetric enforcement; Phase 4 — deploy HMAC verifier removal. Each phase has a go/no-go check. This prevents child 3 from going live before child 6 Phase 2 is complete.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a decision rule co-located with the trust config schema specifying the exact behavior when `env_id` ≠ DSSE `keyid` at verification time (e.g., 'treat keyid as an unauthenticated lookup hint only; verify solely against the pinned key for the required env_id; emit `mismatch` if no pinned key for the required env_id verifies the signature, regardless of keyid').
findings.finding: The trust config schema mandates `env_id` 'MUST equal the DSSE principal (keyid)' but no decision rule or fallback is co-located with the schema for what happens when they differ at verification time. The schema defines this as an invariant but the epic specifies no validation step, error verdict, or fail-closed behavior for the mismatch case — leaving implementers of child 42d1 (trust config + required-environment policy) to independently decide whether to reject the cert with `mismatch`, raise an exception, or silently proceed.
findings.location: Trust config & era-validity protocol section / schema block / AC2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a required `## Rollback` (or `## Back-out`) section to the mandated `docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md` artifact (and update the AC test assertion to check for it), specifying how to temporarily re-enable HMAC acceptance if the contract step must be reverted.
findings.finding: The migration doc AC only mandates `## Expand` and `## Contract` sections but does not require a back-out/rollback procedure for the Contract step itself. Once HMAC is removed (contract phase applied), any consumer still emitting/consuming HMAC op-certs has no stated recovery path. T4 requires the *remedy* be an explicit rollback/back-out plan, not just expand-contract acknowledgement.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — HMAC removal AC (child 6 / migration doc requirement)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the AC for HMAC removal: '…and a `## Rollback` section describing how to temporarily re-enable HMAC acceptance (e.g. by reverting the `hmac_legacy.py` removal commit and the `POLICY` table entry) — a test asserts this section is present.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete spike for the Secrets Manager → KMS-decrypt → ssh-keygen-sign composition path (key format expected, boto3 dependency scope, temp-file materialization strategy) before child 4 is committed to full build-out, or specify these integration details explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'stores the Ed25519 signing key material in AWS Secrets Manager, encrypted at rest by a KMS CMK' but no AWS Secrets Manager integration exists anywhere in the codebase or infra (confirmed: zero hits for aws_secretsmanager_secret in infra/terraform/; boto3 is present only in the reviewbot extra for CloudWatch, not in any mcp extra). The full composition — secretsmanager:GetSecretValue → KMS Decrypt → filesystem materialization → ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <path> — is a novel, unvalidated integration chain. The existing sshsig.sign() requires a key_path filesystem argument; the plan never specifies how the in-memory secret from Secrets Manager is bridged to that path in the trusted server environment. No spike or proof-of-concept is referenced.
findings.location: AC5 (trusted rebar-mcp remote path) / 'stores the Ed25519 signing key material in AWS Secrets Manager, encrypted at rest by a KMS CMK' / Intended decomposition child 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the new MCP-server IaC either extends the existing `infra/terraform/` module (inheriting its remote S3 backend + `use_lockfile=true`) or declares an explicit remote-state backend config for a new root module; add a plan-before-apply CI step (e.g., `terraform plan` gate) for any apply that touches the signing-key secret or IAM resources.
findings.finding: The plan mandates 'IaC for the trusted MCP-server deployment' (AC5, child 4) but does not state whether the new MCP-server resources live inside the existing `infra/terraform/` module (which has a correctly configured S3 remote-state backend with `use_lockfile=true` and `required_version >= 1.10`) or a separate new module. If a new module, no remote-state backend, state locking, or plan-before-apply discipline is specified for it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC5 / Intended decomposition child 4: 'Trusted rebar-mcp remote-execution path … + its AWS infra'; child ticket ee0b AC2
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to child 4 / AC5: 'IaC is delivered as Terraform under `infra/terraform/` (or a new root `infra/opcert-server/` with the same S3 backend + `use_lockfile=true` as `infra/terraform/versions.tf`) and is gated by the existing `terraform-drift.yml` CI plan job before any apply.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the dual-write rule for the expand window in AC6 / `hmac-opcert-removal.md`: state that op-cert writers MUST switch to asymmetric signing at the START of the expand phase (writer-first), and that the contract phase gate condition requires zero HMAC-signed certs in the merged log since the writer cutover date — so no new HMAC certs can arrive after the sweep.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify what cert type (HMAC vs. asymmetric) is produced by the writer during the expand window, leaving the dual-write scenario unaddressed: new op-certs written while both schemes are accepted could be HMAC-signed, requiring a second re-issuance sweep before the contract phase can safely close.
findings.location: AC item 6 / 'Intended decomposition' child 6 / 'Approach — HMAC removal (contract phase)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen child 4214 AC3 to require exhaustive enumeration of ALL `on:` triggers (not just those 'that should fire'), with the `push: branches-ignore: ["feature/**"]` filter explicitly recorded and classified.
findings.finding: Child 4214's CI-trigger audit AC (AC3) requires every 'existing workflow trigger that should fire on the gate' to be classified INCLUDED/NO_FILTER. The live `verify-identity.yaml` has `push: branches-ignore: ["feature/**"]`, which explicitly EXCLUDES feature-branch pushes. Child 4214's AC3 scopes the audit to triggers 'that should fire' — this conditional phrasing allows the `feature/**` exclusion to be silently omitted from the classification comment on the grounds that it 'should not fire.' The parent AC3 is broader: 'enumerate the `on:` triggers … classify each … a comment records the classification so no merge path silently skips the gate.' The child's phrasing narrows the parent's exhaustive enumeration requirement to a conditional one.
findings.location: child 4214-ac28-d19c-41b9, AC3
findings.suggested_fix: Rewrite child 4214 AC3 as: 'ALL `on:` triggers of `verify-identity.yaml` are enumerated and each is classified INCLUDED, NO_FILTER, or EXCLUDED-BY-FILTER (with reason); the `push: branches-ignore: ["feature/**"]` filter is explicitly recorded and its implication for op-cert enforcement on feature branches is noted in a comment.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema-state assertion to AC6 / the `hmac-opcert-removal.md` migration artifact: specify that rolling back the contract phase MUST restore the HMAC verifier (or that all HMAC certs are re-issued before the contract step is applied), and that historical HMAC-signed certs in the merged log remain verifiable after rollback — with a literal DATA LOSS RISK note if they do not.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema/persisted state of the merged-log ledger AFTER a rollback of the HMAC-removal contract phase. If the contract step (removing HMAC verifier code) is partially applied and then rolled back, historical HMAC-signed op-certs already in the merged log may become permanently unverifiable — the plan says rollback 'reverts to local behavior' but only in the context of the required-environment policy toggle, not the signing-scheme migration.
findings.location: AC item 6 (HMAC removal) / 'Rollback' clause at end of AC6
findings.suggested_fix: In AC6, after the rollback clause, add: 'A rollback of the contract phase restores the HMAC verifier path in `attest/registry.py`; the `## Rollback` section of `hmac-opcert-removal.md` MUST assert that all HMAC-signed certs in the merged log are verifiable post-rollback (either because the verifier is restored, or because all were re-issued before the contract step). The contract step MUST NOT be applied until all legacy HMAC certs in the merged log have been re-issued as asymmetric.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an idempotency/resumability requirement to AC6 for the re-issuance step: the `hmac-opcert-removal.md` migration artifact MUST describe how a partial re-issuance run is detected and safely resumed (e.g., by checking whether an asymmetric cert already exists for a given HMAC cert before re-issuing), and a test or procedure step should assert re-running the script on an already-migrated ledger is a no-op.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether the re-issuance of existing HMAC-signed op-certs (if required for the contract phase) is idempotent/resumable — if the re-issue script is interrupted mid-run, it is unspecified whether re-running it is safe or will double-apply transformations to already-migrated entries.
findings.location: AC item 6 / 'expand-contract cutover window / re-issue procedure'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the canonical `material_fingerprint` computation rule (including `ensure_ascii=False`) to the epic's AC4 or 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' section, co-located with the certificate subject binding spec, so the opcert substrate implementer has a normative definition without needing to read `pass1.py`.
findings.finding: The `material_fingerprint` field is referenced in AC4 as a certificate subject binding ('certificates bind {ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position}') and a test is required against it, but the epic plan never defines the computation rule for this field. The definition appears only in child 368c's AC, which describes it as 'the 16-hex-char truncation of sha256(json.dumps({ticket_id, description, file_impact, children}, sort_keys=True))' — but omits `ensure_ascii=False`, which the live implementation (`src/rebar/llm/plan_review/pass1.py:561`) uses. This creates a processing-rule/schema co-location gap: the epic's AC4 binds a field whose computation is both undefined at the epic level and mis-specified at the child level.
findings.location: AC4 / 'Certificates bind {ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position}' / child 368c AC2 / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/pass1.py:561
findings.suggested_fix: In AC4, replace 'material fingerprint' with: 'material fingerprint = the 16-hex-char truncation of SHA-256 over `json.dumps({ticket_id, description, file_impact, children}, sort_keys=True, ensure_ascii=False).encode("utf-8")` (matching `plan_review.pass1.material_fingerprint`; the substrate treats this as an opaque string).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add formal dependency links: 42d1 depends-on 368c; 4214 depends-on 42d1 and 368c; 8f1d depends-on 368c and 42d1.
findings.finding: Child 42d1 explicitly states it consumes the era-validity rule implemented in child 368c, child 4214's gate fixture requires both the opcert kind (368c) and the config loader (42d1) to be operational, and child 8f1d (HMAC removal) must follow the expand phase (which requires 368c + 42d1 to be live). None of these three children declare formal `deps` on their upstream peers. This creates an ordering gap where 42d1, 4214, or 8f1d could be implemented and merged before 368c, producing integration failures.
findings.location: child 42d1-7784-9c82-43f5 (no dep on 368c); child 4214-ac28-d19c-41b9 (no dep on 368c or 42d1); child 8f1d-72d5-d8b1-400f (no dep on 368c or 42d1)
findings.suggested_fix: In the rebar ticket system, set `deps` on 42d1 → 368c, on 4214 → [368c, 42d1], and on 8f1d → [368c, 42d1] to enforce the implementation ordering.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add 'no trusted-clock dependency' as an explicit required element in child 6a89's ADR AC or child 368c's documentation, covering the inherited AC from closed child 5893.
findings.finding: Child 5893 (era-scoped rotation) is closed/superseded by 368c, but its AC2 — 'Rotation is documented; no trusted-clock dependency' — has no explicit coverage in any surviving child. Child 368c covers the implementation and the code comments; child 6a89 covers the ADR. Neither child explicitly commits to documenting that the rotation model has no trusted-clock dependency. This is a residual reference that leaves the documentation sub-criterion orphaned.
findings.location: child 5893-f6d4-f2f3-4542 AC2 vs. surviving siblings
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 6a89 AC1: 'The ADR must document that env-key era validity uses commit-ancestry positions, not wall-clock time, so there is no trusted-clock dependency.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the IaC toolchain for the AWS MCP-server infra in AC5 and child 4 (e.g., 'use Terraform in `infra/opcert-server/`') so an executor does not have to choose.
findings.finding: The AWS infra delivery in AC5 mandates IaC ('IaC for the trusted MCP-server deployment') but never specifies the IaC technology (Terraform, AWS CDK, CloudFormation, Pulumi, etc.). An executing agent would have to choose, which is a real decision — different tools have different state-management, review, and CI-integration consequences that could conflict with the repo's existing conventions.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 / Scope bullet 'IaC for the trusted MCP-server deployment'
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to AC5: 'IaC is delivered as Terraform (or <named tool>) under `infra/opcert-server/`.' Pick whatever matches the repo's existing convention.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a required doc artifact (e.g. `docs/ops/mcp-server-deployment.md`) for the trusted MCP server AWS infrastructure to child 4's scope and the Docs+ADR AC, covering at minimum: IAM policy grants, Secrets Manager key lifecycle (creation, rotation, revocation), and SigV4 endpoint access control — so operational procedures are durable and auditable.
findings.finding: The plan introduces non-trivial AWS security infrastructure (IAM SigV4 endpoint auth, least-privilege IAM policy, Ed25519 key in Secrets Manager encrypted by KMS CMK, rebar-mcp server deployment) but requires no corresponding operational doc/runbook artifact — only the ADR, which covers architectural decisions, not deployment procedures.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (trusted rebar-mcp remote path + AWS infra); Intended decomposition child 4

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step to child 6 (HMAC removal) and its AC requiring an audit of existing docs/ADRs for references to `hmac_legacy.py`, the HMAC op-cert scheme, or symmetric operation certificates, and updating or cross-referencing them to reflect the asymmetric replacement.
findings.finding: The plan removes `src/rebar/attest/hmac_legacy.py` (and its POLICY table entry) but does not require auditing or updating existing docs, ADRs, or comments that reference the HMAC scheme or `hmac_legacy.py` — these will become stale references after the contract step.
findings.location: Scope — 'HMAC removal (contract phase)'; AC — HMAC removal AC; Intended decomposition child 6
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the HMAC removal AC: 'A search of `docs/` and `src/` for references to `hmac_legacy`, `hmac_opcert`, or the legacy symmetric signing scheme confirms all stale references are updated or annotated (a test or CI lint step asserts no live doc references the deleted file).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a concrete spike/proof-of-concept for the AWS infra composition (Secrets Manager retrieve → KMS-envelope-decrypt → in-process SSHSIG Ed25519 sign) before full build-out of child 4, validating key format compatibility, IAM permission model, and signing round-trip.
findings.finding: The AWS infra composition path — retrieve Ed25519 key material from Secrets Manager, KMS-envelope-decrypt it, then perform in-process SSHSIG signing — is a novel integration the plan asserts correct but does not validate with a spike or prototype before full build-out.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC5 (trusted rebar-mcp remote path); 'Intended decomposition' child 4
findings.suggested_fix: Before child 4 implementation begins, write a standalone script (spike) that: (1) stores a test Ed25519 private key in Secrets Manager with a KMS CMK, (2) retrieves and decrypts it from an IAM role matching the planned least-privilege policy, (3) performs an SSHSIG sign + verify round-trip. Record the key format (OpenSSH PKCS8 etc.) and latency. Reference this spike result in the child 4 design doc.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Before child 3 begins, do a brief interface review of the shipped `verify-identity.yaml` walker (Python entry point + callable surface) and document the planned extension seam (e.g., 'walker exports a `walk_merged_log(callback)` hook' or 'CI YAML calls a shared script with a plugin arg') so the 'extend not fork' goal has a concrete implementation path.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the shipped `verify-identity.yaml` merged-log walker is designed for extension (to add the op-cert verification lane) without a prior spike or interface review confirming the walker is factored for extension rather than requiring a fork/copy.
findings.location: Scope section; AC3; 'Intended decomposition' child 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short design note to child 3's spec identifying the specific callable/hook in the shipped walker that will be reused, and whether a refactor of that walker is in-scope for child 3. If no such hook exists yet, explicitly scope a small walker-factoring step into child 3.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a break-glass rollback procedure for the CI gate op-cert lane (e.g., a feature flag env-var, a separate job that can be independently disabled, or a documented one-step revert) so a buggy check can be disabled quickly without disabling the full identity gate.
findings.finding: The CI gate extension (adding the op-cert lane to `.github/workflows/verify-identity.yaml`) has no stated break-glass or rollback procedure: if the added op-cert check contains a bug it could block all merges, and the plan specifies no cheap way to disable just the op-cert lane without touching the entire identity gate.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 / Intended decomposition child 3
findings.suggested_fix: Implement the op-cert check as a separate, independently-togglable job (or a conditional step gated on a repo-level env var `OPCERT_GATE_ENABLED`) so it can be disabled in seconds without touching the identity gate; document this in the migration artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a rollout/rollback specification for the trusted `rebar-mcp` AWS service to child 4 (e.g., blue/green or canary deploy, IaC state rollback steps, and a stated maximum time-to-rollback), so a bad server update has a tested, cheap undo path.
findings.finding: The trusted `rebar-mcp` AWS server is a continuously-deployed long-running service, but the plan specifies no deploy ordering, staged rollout, or rollback procedure for the server itself — only its steady-state IaC config (IAM, Secrets Manager, KMS).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 / Intended decomposition child 4 / Scope ('IaC for the trusted MCP-server deployment')

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the merged-log position bound in the certificate is the commit SHA resolved at the time of the `git fetch` / checkout (the SHA the gate actually ran against), not the HEAD re-read at signing time — and that the signing step receives this SHA as an explicit input from the gate runner, not by re-resolving HEAD.
findings.finding: The plan's integrity requirement ('server MUST fetch authoritative state itself and run the gate on THAT') does not specify which commit SHA gets bound in the certificate when `main` advances between the server's fetch and its signing moment. The plan says the cert binds 'the `main` commit SHA at which the trusted environment ran the gate', but if the server re-reads `HEAD` at signing time (after the gate ran), a race could bind a SHA that differs from the one actually checked out and verified — meaning the cert would claim to attest state at commit C but was actually run against state at C-1.
findings.location: Approach section ('Trusted remote execution for high assurance') and AC4 ('Certificates bind {ticket id, material fingerprint, merged-log position (a main commit SHA)}')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Trust config & era-validity protocol or AC4: 'The server resolves and records the `main` HEAD commit SHA immediately before running the gate on the fetched state; this SHA is passed explicitly to the signing step and becomes the cert's bound merged-log position. HEAD is not re-read at sign time.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the key-materialization strategy in AC5/child 4: state whether `sshsig.sign()` will be extended to accept key bytes (e.g., writing to a named pipe or memory-backed fd), or whether the key is materialized to a memory-backed tmpfs/memfd path with guaranteed cleanup, so the implementation is unambiguous and the Secrets Manager protection is end-to-end.
findings.finding: The plan asserts the trusted rebar-mcp server 'performs SSHSIG signing itself' with the key retrieved from Secrets Manager, but the existing `sshsig.sign()` interface (sshsig.py line 74-86) takes a `key_path: str` filesystem path — it shells out to `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f <key_path>`. There is no in-memory signing path in the shipped substrate. Bridging Secrets Manager key material to this interface requires writing the plaintext private key to a temp file on the server's filesystem (at minimum transiently). The plan never specifies whether a memory-backed location (tmpfs, memfd) is used, whether the file is zeroed before unlink, or how crash-safety is handled — leaving the key-materialization implementation undefined. This is an unverified capability assertion: the signing interface as written cannot sign from in-memory bytes.
findings.location: AC5 (trusted rebar-mcp remote path) / src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py lines 74-86

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to child 4 / AC5 an explicit statement of the admin access path to the new MCP server (e.g., 'administrative shell access is exclusively via AWS SSM Session Manager; no inbound port 22 is permitted, mirroring the Gerrit host pattern').
findings.finding: The plan specifies AWS IAM SigV4 as the endpoint authentication mechanism for developer/client calls to the new trusted rebar-mcp server, but does not state how human administrators (operators) gain shell or console access to the new server process for maintenance, key rotation, or incident response.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC5 / child ticket ee0b AC2(a): 'endpoint authentication via AWS IAM SigV4 restricted to a named admin role over TLS'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a `prevent_destroy = true` lifecycle rule (and/or AWS Secrets Manager `recovery_window_in_days` > 0) to the Secrets Manager secret holding the Ed25519 signing key in the MCP-server IaC, mirroring the existing `prevent_destroy` guards on the Gerrit EBS volume and Elastic IP.
findings.finding: The plan places the Ed25519 signing key — the root of trust for all high-security op-certs — in AWS Secrets Manager but specifies no deletion protection (`prevent_destroy` in Terraform or Secrets Manager's recovery-window / deletion protection flag). Accidental `terraform destroy` or a future IaC misconfiguration could permanently delete the secret.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC5 / child ticket ee0b AC2(c): 'the Ed25519 signing key material stored in AWS Secrets Manager (KMS-CMK-encrypted at rest)'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Terraform resource for the Secrets Manager secret: add `lifecycle { prevent_destroy = true }` and set `recovery_window_in_days = 30` (the maximum) to ensure a deletion cannot be immediately permanent.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement to child 4 / AC5: specify at minimum CloudWatch log shipping for the MCP server process, a health/availability alarm (analogous to the existing `rebar-gerrit-gate-down` alarm), and an alarm on signing-key access failures (Secrets Manager GetSecretValue errors).
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new network-reachable AWS service (the trusted rebar-mcp signing server) but specifies no observability — no CloudWatch logging, metrics, or alarms — for the new infra. The existing IaC has comprehensive monitoring (monitoring.tf, monitoring_1fa8.tf, monitoring_88ab.tf, monitoring_autodeploy.tf, monitoring_autolander.tf, etc.); the new MCP server is completely dark in the plan.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC5 / Scope / Intended decomposition child 4: 'Trusted rebar-mcp remote-execution path … + its AWS infra'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the credential-issuance mechanism for developer clients calling the rebar-mcp SigV4 endpoint (e.g. IAM Identity Center/SSO short-lived session, OIDC-federated role assumption, or explicit rationale if static keys are used with compensating controls); add this to the IaC spec in child ee0b.
findings.finding: The plan declares the rebar-mcp endpoint requires 'AWS IAM (SigV4-signed requests) restricted to a named admin role' but does not specify how developer clients obtain IAM credentials to make SigV4-signed calls (IAM Identity Center/SSO, OIDC-federated role assumption, or static long-lived IAM user access keys). Static long-lived access keys are a common implementation path here and represent a secret-lifecycle risk on the boundary-crossing path; the credential-issuance mechanism for callers is undeclared.
findings.location: AC — trusted rebar-mcp path (AC bullet 5 / child ee0b); 'AWS IAM (SigV4-signed requests) restricted to a named admin role, over TLS'
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that callers use IAM Identity Center (AWS SSO) short-lived session credentials or OIDC role assumption (e.g. via `aws sts assume-role` with MFA, or a CI OIDC trust) so no static long-lived access keys are issued. Add a line to the AC: 'caller credentials are short-lived session tokens obtained via IAM Identity Center or OIDC role-assumption; no static long-lived IAM access keys are issued for MCP endpoint callers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to child ee0b's IaC/implementation spec a concrete contract for transient key-material handling: e.g. read from Secrets Manager into a tmpfs-backed or in-memory path, passed to ssh-keygen via a named pipe or O_TMPFILE, with explicit shredding after signing, and no key material written to persistent storage or logged.
findings.finding: The plan stores the Ed25519 signing key in AWS Secrets Manager but does not specify how the private key material is transiently delivered to the SSHSIG subprocess on the server. The existing sshsig.py implementation signs via `ssh-keygen -Y sign -f key_path` — the `-f` flag requires a filesystem path, so the server must write the key material to disk (or a tmpfile) before signing. The plan is silent on this handling: whether the key is written to a tmpfs/memfd-backed path, a 0600 tmpfile that is immediately shredded, or (risk) follows the existing pattern of materializing secrets into /etc/rebar/.env. Key-material-on-disk or in process-list arguments (which the existing ssm.tf/user_data.sh pattern writes to a file) is a secret-lifecycle exposure on the boundary-crossing path.
findings.location: AC bullet 5 (child ee0b): 'the server performs SSHSIG signing itself with that key'; Approach section: 'that server MUST fetch the authoritative state itself … and run the gate on THAT'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the epic's 'Era-validity decision rule' subsection with the complete two-phase verdict table: (1) any-historical-key verifies AND era-scoped key verifies → `certified`; (2) any-historical-key verifies BUT no era-scoped key verifies → `key_not_valid_at_era`; (3) no historical key verifies → `mismatch`; so children have a single normative source without reading child 368c.
findings.finding: The two-phase verdict decision rule (verify against any historical key → if passes but era fails → `key_not_valid_at_era`; else → ?) is stated in child 368c but the epic plan's 'Trust config & era-validity protocol' section — which is explicitly designated as 'the single schema children 368c/42d1/4214/ee0b/8f1d all read' — only describes the era-validity rule for the positive (valid) case and for `key_not_valid_at_era`. The fallback verdict for the case where the any-historical-key pass itself fails (i.e., the signature is not by any known key) is never named in the epic body. This is a processing-protocol/decision-rule gap: the normative section is incomplete relative to the full state space.
findings.location: Trust config & era-validity protocol section / Era-validity decision rule subsection / AC4
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Era-validity decision rule' section: 'Two-phase verdict: (1) verify against ALL historical keys (including revoked); if none verifies → `mismatch`; (2) if step 1 passes, verify against only currently-valid (era-scoped) keys; if none → `key_not_valid_at_era`; if step 2 passes → `certified`. All three are in the canonical `verify_signature_result.schema.json` enum.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly assign the expand-phase code implementation (dual-acceptance: verify both HMAC and sshsig for op-cert kinds) to child 8f1d or a new child, and add acceptance criteria for it.
findings.finding: The expand phase of the HMAC expand-contract migration — the dual-acceptance window during which both HMAC and asymmetric op-certs are simultaneously verified — is documented in child 8f1d's migration artifact (the `## Expand` section) but no child is assigned to implement the dual-acceptance code. Child 8f1d is scoped to the contract phase (removal). The parent's decomposition lists 'HMAC removal (contract)' as child 6 but never assigns the expand-phase implementation to any child. This is an owned-by-none gap.
findings.location: parent decomposition child 6 (8f1d) + sibling roster
findings.suggested_fix: Extend child 8f1d AC to include: 'During the expand window, `registry.POLICY` maps op-cert kinds to accept both HMAC-SHA256 (legacy) and sshsig (new) schemes; a test asserts both pass during the expand window.' Add a concrete cutover step.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a shared typed interface (TypedDict or dataclass) for the parsed environment key record in a module owned by 368c (e.g. `opcert.py`) that 42d1 imports and returns from its loader, with a contract test asserting round-trip fidelity.
findings.finding: Children 368c and 42d1 both process the `.rebar/trusted_environments.yaml` file — 368c implements the era-validity rule that consumes `added_at_commit`/`revoked_at_commit` fields from parsed key records, while 42d1 owns the config loader/parser that produces those records — but no shared schema type, typed interface, or validation artifact is defined between them. If 42d1's loader produces a different dict shape than 368c's era-check expects (e.g. different key names, missing fields, different null representation), the integration silently fails with no contract to catch the mismatch.
findings.location: child 368c-1c88-3999-4fbd context + child 42d1-7784-9c82-43f5 context
findings.suggested_fix: Add to child 368c's deliverables: a `EnvKeyRecord` TypedDict (or dataclass) with fields `public_key: str`, `added_at_commit: str`, `revoked_at_commit: str | None`; child 42d1's loader returns `list[EnvKeyRecord]` and is tested against the schema.

findings.finding: The session log names an explicit design session titled 'Security/OSS-alignment assessment: identity impl + op-cert plan vs gittuf/TUF/Sigstore/in-toto/SLSA', implying requirements or decisions emerged about how op-certs relate to/differ from those external frameworks. The plan's ADR acceptance criteria do not require documenting this alignment (they reference only ADR 0046, DSSE, OpenSSH, RFC 8725), so any alignment decisions or constraints expressed in that session are silently absent from the ADR deliverable.

`bad_signature` is introduced as a new op-cert verdict by the two-phase protocol in child 368c but is absent from the canonical `verify_signature_result.schema.json` verdict enum, and the string form (`bad_signature` underscored vs `bad-signature` hyphenated used in the existing authorship gate) is never resolved in the plan.
findings.location: AC4 / child 368c description ('two-phase verdict') / src/rebar/schemas/verify_signature_result.schema.json
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"bad-signature"` to the schema enum (matching `BAD_SIGNATURE = "bad-signature"` in `verify_authorship.py`), add it to `src/rebar/types.py`'s `VerifySignatureResult.verdict` Literal, and update child 368c to use the hyphenated form consistently.

findings.finding: AC6's test assertion 'names the cutover version' references an undefined version: the test for `docs/migrations/hmac-opcert-removal.md` asserts the file 'names the cutover version', but the plan never defines what the cutover version is, how it is determined, or what naming convention applies (semantic version? git tag? milestone label?). An executor cannot write a passing test or the document without this definition.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC6: 'a test asserts the file exists, is non-empty, and names the cutover version'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'names the cutover version' with 'names the version string `rebar-opcert-asymmetric-1.0` (or the project's release tag) at which HMAC op-certs are no longer accepted, with that string hardcoded as the test's expected value.

findings.finding: AC3's 'every existing workflow trigger that should fire on the gate is classified INCLUDED/NO_FILTER' has no enumeration basis or classification protocol: the plan does not state what 'INCLUDED/NO_FILTER' means, who defines the reference list of triggers 'that should fire', or what artifact records the classification. An executor cannot determine when this assertion is complete or write a test for it.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'every existing workflow trigger that should fire on the gate is classified INCLUDED/NO_FILTER'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
